question:
A-4. An unbiased coin is tossed \( n \) times. What is the expected value of \( |H-T| \), where \( H \) is the number of heads and \( T \) is the number of tails? In other words, evaluate in closed form:
\[
\frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \sum_{k<n / 2}(n-2 k)\binom{n}{k} .
\]
(In this problem, "closed form" means a form not involving a series. The given series can be reduced to a single term involving only binomial coefficients, rational functions of \( n \) and \( 2^{n} \), and the greatest integer function \( [x] \).)
solution:
A-4.
The answer is
\[
\frac{n}{2^{n-1}}\binom{n-1}{[(n-1) / 2]}
\]
since
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k<n / 2}(n-2 k)\binom{n}{k} & =\sum_{k<n / 2}\left\{(n-k)\binom{n}{k}-k\binom{n}{k}\right\} \\
& =\sum_{k<n / 2}\left\{n\binom{n-1}{k}-n\binom{n-1}{k-1}\right\}=n \sum_{k<n / 2}\left\{\binom{n-1}{k}-\binom{n-1}{k-1}\right\} \\
& =n\binom{n-1}{[(n-1) / 2]} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
A-4. An unbiased coin is tossed \( trials \) times. What is the expected value of \( |headsct - tailsct| \), where \( headsct \) is the number of heads and \( tailsct \) is the number of tails? In other words, evaluate in closed form:
\[
\frac{1}{2^{trials-1}} \sum_{chooser<trials / 2}(trials-2 chooser)\binom{trials}{chooser} .
\]
(In this problem, "closed form" means a form not involving a series. The given series can be reduced to a single term involving only binomial coefficients, rational functions of \( trials \) and \( 2^{trials} \), and the greatest integer function \( [x] \).)
descriptive_long.solution:
A-4.
The answer is
\[
\frac{trials}{2^{trials-1}}\binom{trials-1}{[(trials-1) / 2]}
\]
since
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{chooser<trials / 2}(trials-2 chooser)\binom{trials}{chooser} & =\sum_{chooser<trials / 2}\left\{(trials-chooser)\binom{trials}{chooser}-chooser\binom{trials}{chooser}\right\} \\
& =\sum_{chooser<trials / 2}\left\{trials\binom{trials-1}{chooser}-trials\binom{trials-1}{chooser-1}\right\}=trials \sum_{chooser<trials / 2}\left\{\binom{trials-1}{chooser}-\binom{trials-1}{chooser-1}\right\} \\
& =trials\binom{trials-1}{[(trials-1) / 2]} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-4. An unbiased coin is tossed \( centipede \) times. What is the expected value of \( |blueberry-sunflower| \), where \( blueberry \) is the number of heads and \( sunflower \) is the number of tails? In other words, evaluate in closed form:
\[
\frac{1}{2^{centipede-1}} \sum_{harmonic<centipede / 2}(centipede-2 harmonic)\binom{centipede}{harmonic} .
\]
(In this problem, "closed form" means a form not involving a series. The given series can be reduced to a single term involving only binomial coefficients, rational functions of \( centipede \) and \( 2^{centipede} \), and the greatest integer function \( [x] \).)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-4.
The answer is
\[
\frac{centipede}{2^{centipede-1}}\binom{centipede-1}{[(centipede-1) / 2]}
\]
since
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{harmonic<centipede / 2}(centipede-2 harmonic)\binom{centipede}{harmonic} & =\sum_{harmonic<centipede / 2}\left\{(centipede-harmonic)\binom{centipede}{harmonic}-harmonic\binom{centipede}{harmonic}\right\} \\
& =\sum_{harmonic<centipede / 2}\left\{centipede\binom{centipede-1}{harmonic}-centipede\binom{centipede-1}{harmonic-1}\right\}=centipede \sum_{harmonic<centipede / 2}\left\{\binom{centipede-1}{harmonic}-\binom{centipede-1}{harmonic-1}\right\} \\
& =centipede\binom{centipede-1}{[(centipede-1) / 2]} .
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-4. An unbiased coin is tossed \( zeroings \) times. What is the expected value of \(|tailscount-headscount|\), where \( tailscount \) is the number of heads and \( headscount \) is the number of tails? In other words, evaluate in closed form:
\[
\frac{1}{2^{zeroings-1}} \sum_{complete<zeroings / 2}(zeroings-2 complete)\binom{zeroings}{complete} .
\]
(In this problem, "closed form" means a form not involving a series. The given series can be reduced to a single term involving only binomial coefficients, rational functions of \( zeroings \) and \( 2^{zeroings} \), and the greatest integer function \( [x] \).)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-4.
The answer is
\[
\frac{zeroings}{2^{zeroings-1}}\binom{zeroings-1}{[(zeroings-1) / 2]}
\]
since
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{complete<zeroings / 2}(zeroings-2 complete)\binom{zeroings}{complete} & =\sum_{complete<zeroings / 2}\left\{(zeroings-complete)\binom{zeroings}{complete}-complete\binom{zeroings}{complete}\right\} \\
& =\sum_{complete<zeroings / 2}\left\{zeroings\binom{zeroings-1}{complete}-zeroings\binom{zeroings-1}{complete-1}\right\}=zeroings \sum_{complete<zeroings / 2}\left\{\binom{zeroings-1}{complete}-\binom{zeroings-1}{complete-1}\right\} \\
& =zeroings\binom{zeroings-1}{[(zeroings-1) / 2]} .
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
A-4. An unbiased coin is tossed \( xqrvzlpa \) times. What is the expected value of \( |mbczrlyo-gjksnuqe| \), where \( mbczrlyo \) is the number of heads and \( gjksnuqe \) is the number of tails? In other words, evaluate in closed form:
\[
\frac{1}{2^{xqrvzlpa-1}} \sum_{hvydpqom<xqrvzlpa / 2}(xqrvzlpa-2 hvydpqom)\binom{xqrvzlpa}{hvydpqom} .
\]
(In this problem, "closed form" means a form not involving a series. The given series can be reduced to a single term involving only binomial coefficients, rational functions of \( xqrvzlpa \) and \( 2^{xqrvzlpa} \), and the greatest integer function \( [x] \).)
garbled_string.solution:
A-4.
The answer is
\[
\frac{xqrvzlpa}{2^{xqrvzlpa-1}}\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{[(xqrvzlpa-1) / 2]}
\]
since
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{hvydpqom<xqrvzlpa / 2}(xqrvzlpa-2 hvydpqom)\binom{xqrvzlpa}{hvydpqom} & =\sum_{hvydpqom<xqrvzlpa / 2}\left\{(xqrvzlpa-hvydpqom)\binom{xqrvzlpa}{hvydpqom}-hvydpqom\binom{xqrvzlpa}{hvydpqom}\right\} \\
& =\sum_{hvydpqom<xqrvzlpa / 2}\left\{xqrvzlpa\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{hvydpqom}-xqrvzlpa\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{hvydpqom-1}\right\}=xqrvzlpa \sum_{hvydpqom<xqrvzlpa / 2}\left\{\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{hvydpqom}-\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{hvydpqom-1}\right\} \\
& =xqrvzlpa\binom{xqrvzlpa-1}{[(xqrvzlpa-1) / 2]} .
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Two players, A and B, each toss an unbiased coin n times, the two experiments being independent.  
Let H_1 (resp. H_2) be A's (resp. B's) number of heads and set  

  \Delta _n = |H_1 - H_2|.

1.  Show that the expectation  
  E[\Delta _n] = 2^{-2n} \sum _{k=0}^{n} \sum _{\ell =0}^{n} |k-\ell | \(\binom{n}{k}\)\(\binom{n}{\ell }\)  
   has the closed form  
   E[\Delta _n]= n\cdot 2^{-(2n-1)} \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\).

2.  Deduce the binomial identity  
   \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\).

(You must give a result that involves no summations over k or \ell  and contains only elementary functions of n and a single binomial coefficient.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Expressing the expectation  
Because every pair (H_1,H_2)=(k,\ell ) occurs with probability \(2^{-2n}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }\),

 E[\Delta _n]=2^{-2n} \sum _{k=0}^{n} \sum _{\ell =0}^{n}|k-\ell |\(\binom{n}{k}\)\(\binom{n}{\ell }\). (1)

Step 2 - Symmetry reduction  
Since |k-\ell |=|\ell -k|,

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }
  =2 \sum _{0\leq k<\ell \leq n}(\ell -k)\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }. (2)

Step 3 - Introducing the distance j=\ell -k  
Write j=\ell -k (1\leq j\leq n) and keep k.  Then (2) becomes  

 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \sum _{k=0}^{\,n-j}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{k+j}. (3)

Step 4 - Evaluating the inner sum (correct Vandermonde use)  
Because k+j\leq n, we may replace \(\binom{n}{k+j}\) by \(\binom{n}{\,n-(k+j)}\).  
Set m=n-j.  Then

 \sum _{k=0}^{\,n-j}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{k+j}
 =\sum _{k=0}^{m}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{m-k} (with m=n-j)  
 = \(\binom{2n}{m}\) (by the classical Vandermonde convolution)  
 = \(\binom{2n}{\,n-j}\)=\(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\) (by symmetry r\mapsto 2n-r). (4)

Insert (4) into (3):

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }
  = 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\). (5)

Denote  

 S_n:=\sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\). (6)

Step 5 - A telescoping evaluation of S_n  
We claim  

 j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n\,[\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\) - \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\)]. (7)

Proof of (7).  Using \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}=\dfrac{2n}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\),

 j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)=\dfrac{2nj}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\)
 = n\!\left[\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\right],  

since  
\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\)=\dfrac{n-j}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\).  \square 

Insert (7) into (6):

 S_n = n \sum _{j=1}^{n}\left[\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\right]. (8)

The inner sum telescopes:

 \sum _{j=1}^{n}\bigl(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\bigr)
 = \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}\) - \(\binom{2n-1}{\,2n}\)=\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}\). (9)

(Here \(\binom{2n-1}{\,2n}\)=0.)  
Because \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}=\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\) the final form is

 S_n = n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (10)

Step 6 - Completing the expectation  
From (5) and (10)

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }=2 S_n
  = 2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (11)

Insert (11) into (1):

 E[\Delta _n] = 2^{-2n}\cdot 2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\)
    = n 2^{-(2n-1)} \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (12)

This is the required closed form with a single binomial coefficient.

Step 7 - The announced binomial identity  
Equation (5) together with (10) reads

 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)=2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\),

and dividing by 2 gives

 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\),

which is exactly the identity requested in part 2. \square

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimensionality: the original problem involved one random variable; the new variant involves the joint distribution of two independent binomial variables, producing a genuine two–dimensional double sum.  
• Additional combinatorial structure: evaluation requires both a telescoping argument and the Chu–Vandermonde convolution, an identity that is unnecessary in the original one–dimensional case.  
• Deeper techniques: the proof of the single–sum formula (8) makes use of generating functions (or a slick algebraic manipulation of binomial coefficients) beyond the elementary telescoping used originally.  
• More steps: the solution passes through symmetry reduction, index transformation, convolution, and a generating–function argument before arriving at the closed form, whereas the original problem is settled by a single elementary telescoping.  
Thus the enhanced variant is significantly more technically demanding while preserving the core idea of “distance between two binomial counts.”

original_kernel_variant.question:
Two players, A and B, each toss an unbiased coin n times, the two experiments being independent.  
Let H_1 (resp. H_2) be A's (resp. B's) number of heads and set  

  \Delta _n = |H_1 - H_2|.

1.  Show that the expectation  
  E[\Delta _n] = 2^{-2n} \sum _{k=0}^{n} \sum _{\ell =0}^{n} |k-\ell | \(\binom{n}{k}\)\(\binom{n}{\ell }\)  
   has the closed form  
   E[\Delta _n]= n\cdot 2^{-(2n-1)} \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\).

2.  Deduce the binomial identity  
   \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\).

(You must give a result that involves no summations over k or \ell  and contains only elementary functions of n and a single binomial coefficient.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Expressing the expectation  
Because every pair (H_1,H_2)=(k,\ell ) occurs with probability \(2^{-2n}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }\),

 E[\Delta _n]=2^{-2n} \sum _{k=0}^{n} \sum _{\ell =0}^{n}|k-\ell |\(\binom{n}{k}\)\(\binom{n}{\ell }\). (1)

Step 2 - Symmetry reduction  
Since |k-\ell |=|\ell -k|,

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }
  =2 \sum _{0\leq k<\ell \leq n}(\ell -k)\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }. (2)

Step 3 - Introducing the distance j=\ell -k  
Write j=\ell -k (1\leq j\leq n) and keep k.  Then (2) becomes  

 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \sum _{k=0}^{\,n-j}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{k+j}. (3)

Step 4 - Evaluating the inner sum (correct Vandermonde use)  
Because k+j\leq n, we may replace \(\binom{n}{k+j}\) by \(\binom{n}{\,n-(k+j)}\).  
Set m=n-j.  Then

 \sum _{k=0}^{\,n-j}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{k+j}
 =\sum _{k=0}^{m}\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{m-k} (with m=n-j)  
 = \(\binom{2n}{m}\) (by the classical Vandermonde convolution)  
 = \(\binom{2n}{\,n-j}\)=\(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\) (by symmetry r\mapsto 2n-r). (4)

Insert (4) into (3):

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }
  = 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\). (5)

Denote  

 S_n:=\sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\). (6)

Step 5 - A telescoping evaluation of S_n  
We claim  

 j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n\,[\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\) - \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\)]. (7)

Proof of (7).  Using \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}=\dfrac{2n}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\),

 j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)=\dfrac{2nj}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\)
 = n\!\left[\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\right],  

since  
\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\)=\dfrac{n-j}{n+j}\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}\).  \square 

Insert (7) into (6):

 S_n = n \sum _{j=1}^{n}\left[\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\right]. (8)

The inner sum telescopes:

 \sum _{j=1}^{n}\bigl(\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j-1}-\binom{2n-1}{\,n+j}\bigr)
 = \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}\) - \(\binom{2n-1}{\,2n}\)=\(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}\). (9)

(Here \(\binom{2n-1}{\,2n}\)=0.)  
Because \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n}=\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\) the final form is

 S_n = n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (10)

Step 6 - Completing the expectation  
From (5) and (10)

 \sum _{k,\ell }|k-\ell |\binom{n}{k}\binom{n}{\ell }=2 S_n
  = 2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (11)

Insert (11) into (1):

 E[\Delta _n] = 2^{-2n}\cdot 2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\)
    = n 2^{-(2n-1)} \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\). (12)

This is the required closed form with a single binomial coefficient.

Step 7 - The announced binomial identity  
Equation (5) together with (10) reads

 2 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)=2n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\),

and dividing by 2 gives

 \sum _{j=1}^{n} j \(\binom{2n}{\,n+j}\)= n \(\binom{2n-1}{\,n-1}\),

which is exactly the identity requested in part 2. \square
